title: Swollen or Asymmetrical Testicles in Cats: Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Notice your cat's testicles are swollen or uneven? Learn about potential causes, the medium urgency level, and how to help your vet with triage.
content_html:
What Is It?

When one or both testicles in a cat appear larger than usual or asymmetrical, it typically indicates inflammation, infection, or abnormal tissue growth. Common conditions include orchitis (inflammation of the testes), epididymitis (inflammation of the sperm duct), or testicular tumors. It could also be the result of physical trauma, an insect sting, or a localized abscess caused by a bite wound.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for testicular swelling is generally considered Medium. While it is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is often quite painful and can lead to permanent damage or systemic infection if left untreated. You should aim to have your cat seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. If your cat is also showing signs of lethargy, vomiting, or a high fever, you should seek care sooner.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the affected area can significantly assist a veterinary professional in performing a remote triage. A photo allows the vet to see if the skin is discolored, if there are visible puncture wounds from a fight, or if the swelling is localized to the skin versus the underlying organs. This information helps them determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen and whether a specialized exam is required.

faqs.question: Could it be a tumor?
faqs.answer: Yes, testicular tumors can cause asymmetry and swelling, though they are more common in older, intact male cats.

faqs.question: Is my cat in pain if the area is swollen?
faqs.answer: Most likely, yes. Testicular inflammation is usually very sensitive. You may notice your cat licking the area excessively or reacting defensively when touched.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human pain medication?
faqs.answer: No. Never give a cat human pain relievers like Tylenol or Ibuprofen, as these are highly toxic to felines. Wait for a veterinarian to prescribe safe medication.
